syntax_2903.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2903.title: Define Condition
syntax_2903.description:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to create custom conditions.

Use the first pattern if you want to create a condition with only one pattern, and use the second pattern for multiple patterns (then `patterns` section is used to enter the patterns).

Use the last pattern to create the following patterns easily.
```skript
%skript types% (is|are) <pattern>
%skript types% (isn't|is not|aren't|are not) <pattern>
```

---
The `local` part on the patterns makes the condition local to the current script, so it can't be used from any other script.

(Optional) a `parse` section can be used to run some code at the parse time of this condition. If this section is included, you must also [continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the condition was parsed successfully. Local variables created in this section are copied by-value to other sections.

After all, a `check` section must exist. Do the actual process in this section and [Continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the condition will be positive.

Also check out the [Negate Condition](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2913) effect.

en:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to create custom conditions.

Use the first pattern if you want to create a condition with only one pattern, and use the second pattern for multiple patterns (then `patterns` section is used to enter the patterns).

Use the last pattern to create the following patterns easily.
```skript
%skript types% (is|are) <pattern>
%skript types% (isn't|is not|aren't|are not) <pattern>
```

---
The `local` part on the patterns makes the condition local to the current script, so it can't be used from any other script.

(Optional) a `parse` section can be used to run some code at the parse time of this condition. If this section is included, you must also [continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the condition was parsed successfully. Local variables created in this section are copied by-value to other sections.

After all, a `check` section must exist. Do the actual process in this section and [Continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the condition will be positive.

Also check out the [Negate Condition](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2913) effect.

syntax_2904.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2904.title: Define Effect
syntax_2904.description:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to create custom effects.

Use the first pattern if you want to create an effect with only one pattern, and use the second pattern for multiple patterns (then `patterns` section is used to enter the patterns).

---

The `local` part on the patterns makes the effect local to the current script, so it can't be used from any other script.

(Optional) a `parse` section can be used to run some code at the parse time of this effect. If this section is included, you must also [continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the effect was parsed successfully. Local variables created in this section are copied by-value to other sections.

After all, a `trigger` section must exist. You can do the actual process on this section.

Also check out the [Delay Effect](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2911) effect.

en:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to create custom effects.

Use the first pattern if you want to create an effect with only one pattern, and use the second pattern for multiple patterns (then `patterns` section is used to enter the patterns).

---

The `local` part on the patterns makes the effect local to the current script, so it can't be used from any other script.

(Optional) a `parse` section can be used to run some code at the parse time of this effect. If this section is included, you must also [continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the effect was parsed successfully. Local variables created in this section are copied by-value to other sections.

After all, a `trigger` section must exist. You can do the actual process on this section.

Also check out the [Delay Effect](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2911) effect.

syntax_2929.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2929.title: Members
syntax_2929.description:
Returns a list of the fields, methods, or constructors of an object, including their modifiers and parameters.

If you need a list of field or method names without modifier or parameter details, see [Member Names](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2928) expression.

en:
Returns a list of the fields, methods, or constructors of an object, including their modifiers and parameters.

If you need a list of field or method names without modifier or parameter details, see [Member Names](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2928) expression.

syntax_2924.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2924.title: Last Java Error
syntax_2924.description:
Returns the last error object thrown by a [Java call](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2923) if it was suppressed.

If there was an issue resolving the method or converting its output, it may be a [com.btk5h.skriptmirror.JavaCallException](https://github.com/btk5h/skript-mirror/blob/master/src/main/java/com/btk5h/skriptmirror/JavaCallException.java).

en:
Returns the last error object thrown by a [Java call](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2923) if it was suppressed.

If there was an issue resolving the method or converting its output, it may be a [com.btk5h.skriptmirror.JavaCallException](https://github.com/btk5h/skript-mirror/blob/master/src/main/java/com/btk5h/skriptmirror/JavaCallException.java).

syntax_2937.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2937.title: Define Computed Option
syntax_2937.description:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to define computed options.

Skript's options section allows you to create snippets of text that are copied into other sections of your script. This is useful for static text, but does not work well for text that must be derived from dynamic sources, such as variables.

After the computed option is defined, it is accessible as `{@<option name>}`  within the same script.

A `get` section is required. Code in this section is executed as soon as it is parsed. This section must return a value (using the [Return](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2914) effect) and must not contain delays.

en:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to define computed options.

Skript's options section allows you to create snippets of text that are copied into other sections of your script. This is useful for static text, but does not work well for text that must be derived from dynamic sources, such as variables.

After the computed option is defined, it is accessible as `{@<option name>}`  within the same script.

A `get` section is required. Code in this section is executed as soon as it is parsed. This section must return a value (using the [Return](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2914) effect) and must not contain delays.

syntax_2923.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2923.title: Java Call
syntax_2923.description:
Used to run java code.

Click [**here**](https://skript-mirror.gitbook.io/docs/basics/running-java-code#descriptors) to get more info about `descriptor`s.

The patterns that contains double dots are used to call the code dynamically.

The patterns that ends with **`!`** are used to call fields (they end with `!` due to limitations in Skript's parser.).

The last pattern is used to call constructors.

---

Methods or fields can be used as skript conditions, like: `if {entity}.isLeashed():` or as an in-line condition: `{entity}.isLeashed()`

---
Adding **`try`** before a Java call prevents errors from being logged to the console.
If an error occurs, the error object can still be accessed programmatically using the [Last Java Error](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2924) expression.

en:
Used to run java code.

Click [**here**](https://skript-mirror.gitbook.io/docs/basics/running-java-code#descriptors) to get more info about `descriptor`s.

The patterns that contains double dots are used to call the code dynamically.

The patterns that ends with **`!`** are used to call fields (they end with `!` due to limitations in Skript's parser.).

The last pattern is used to call constructors.

---

Methods or fields can be used as skript conditions, like: `if {entity}.isLeashed():` or as an in-line condition: `{entity}.isLeashed()`

---
Adding **`try`** before a Java call prevents errors from being logged to the console.
If an error occurs, the error object can still be accessed programmatically using the [Last Java Error](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2924) expression.

syntax_2925.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2925.title: Class from a Fully Qualified Name

syntax_2915.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2915.title: Array Value
syntax_2915.description:
Represents the value at a certain index of an array.

This value may be read from and written to.

❗ The brackets in this syntax are literal, not representing an optional group.

en:
Represents the value at a certain index of an array.

This value may be read from and written to.

❗ The brackets in this syntax are literal, not representing an optional group.

syntax_2914.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2914.title: Return
syntax_2914.description: Used to return something from a [custom expression](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2905).

en: Used to return something from a [custom expression](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2905).

syntax_2918.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2918.title: Collect
syntax_2918.description:
Creates an array containing the specified objects. Specifying a type determines the component type of the resulting array.

❗ The brackets in this syntax are literal, not representing an optional group.

en:
Creates an array containing the specified objects. Specifying a type determines the component type of the resulting array.

❗ The brackets in this syntax are literal, not representing an optional group.

syntax_2913.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2913.title: Custom Conditions - Negate Condition
syntax_2913.description: Negates the custom condition. For example if the condition continues, it will not continue after using this. Useful for conditions like "is/isn't".

en: Negates the custom condition. For example if the condition continues, it will not continue after using this. Useful for conditions like "is/isn't".

syntax_2911.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2911.title: Custom Effects - Delay Effect
syntax_2911.description: Delays the current effect. Use this when you do slow things in the effect, so it will not continue to running the code below the effect until you continue using the [Continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) effect.

en: Delays the current effect. Use this when you do slow things in the effect, so it will not continue to running the code below the effect until you continue using the [Continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) effect.

syntax_2917.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2917.title: Custom Expressions - Change Values
syntax_2917.description:
Represents the value (or values) that the custom expression is being changed by.

If multiple change values are expected, use the plural form of the expression `change value`**`s`** instead of the singular `change value`.

en:
Represents the value (or values) that the custom expression is being changed by.

If multiple change values are expected, use the plural form of the expression `change value`**`s`** instead of the singular `change value`.
Continues a [delayed custom effect](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2911).

syntax_2920.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2920.title: Custom Syntaxes - Event Classes
syntax_2920.description: Returns classes of the current events in a `parse` section of a custom event. Like [ScriptLoader#getCurrentEvents()](https://github.com/SkriptLang/Skript/blob/606cf1524c92ee6df903d81a50effb85b929cb8e/src/main/java/ch/njol/skript/ScriptLoader.java#L1148).

en: Returns classes of the current events in a `parse` section of a custom event. Like [ScriptLoader#getCurrentEvents()](https://github.com/SkriptLang/Skript/blob/606cf1524c92ee6df903d81a50effb85b929cb8e/src/main/java/ch/njol/skript/ScriptLoader.java#L1148).

syntax_2921.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2921.title: Custom Syntaxes - Expression
syntax_2921.description: Gets the value of an expression of custom syntaxes.

en: Gets the value of an expression of custom syntaxes.

syntax_2927.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2927.title: Custom Syntaxes - Matched Pattern
syntax_2927.description: Returns index (starts from 0) of the matched pattern if the custom syntax has multiple patterns.

en: Returns index (starts from 0) of the matched pattern if the custom syntax has multiple patterns.

syntax_2930.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2930.title: Custom Syntaxes - Parse Mark
syntax_2930.description:
The parse mark of the pattern. By default it is **`0`**.

This value is [XOR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XOR_gate)ed. For example if the pattern is `(1¦foo|2¦bar) (3¦hee|4¦yaw)` and used `bar hee`, the parse mark will be `2` XOR `3` = **`1`**.

en:
The parse mark of the pattern. By default it is **`0`**.

This value is [XOR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XOR_gate)ed. For example if the pattern is `(1¦foo|2¦bar) (3¦hee|4¦yaw)` and used `bar hee`, the parse mark will be `2` XOR `3` = **`1`**.

syntax_2919.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2919.title: Event
syntax_2919.description: Basically returns the current event object.

en: Basically returns the current event object.

syntax_2912.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2912.title: Import
syntax_2912.description:
This effect works in the same way with the [Import](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2906) section, but this is for Skript's effect commands only.

#### Example Usage
```
!import java.util.UUID
!send "%UUID.randomUUID()%"
```

en:
This effect works in the same way with the [Import](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2906) section, but this is for Skript's effect commands only.

#### Example Usage
```
!import java.util.UUID
!send "%UUID.randomUUID()%"
```

syntax_2934.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2934.title: Spread
syntax_2934.description: Returns the contents of a single array, iterable, iterator or stream as a Skript list.

en: Returns the contents of a single array, iterable, iterator or stream as a Skript list.

syntax_2909.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2909.title: Is Instance of
syntax_2909.description: The same with the `instanceof` operator in Java.
syntax_2909.syntax_pattern:
%objects% (is|are) [a[n]] instance[s] of %javatypes%
%objects% (is not|isn't|are not|aren't) [a[n]] instance[s] of %javatypes%

en: The same with the `instanceof` operator in Java.

syntax_2928.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2928.title: Member Names
syntax_2928.description: Returns a list of the the fields or methods of an object.

en: Returns a list of the the fields or methods of an object.

syntax_2936.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2936.title: Null
syntax_2936.description: Represents `null` in Java. This is different from Skript's `<none>`.

en: Represents `null` in Java. This is different from Skript's `<none>`.

syntax_2933.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2933.title: Custom Syntaxes - Raw Expression
syntax_2933.description: Returns the underlying object of an [expression](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2921).

en: Returns the underlying object of an [expression](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2921).

syntax_2916.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2916.title: Bits
syntax_2916.description:
Represents a subset of bits from a number.

This value may be read from and written to

en:
Represents a subset of bits from a number.

This value may be read from and written to

syntax_2931.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2931.title: Custom Syntaxes - Parse Regex
syntax_2931.description:
The text input for a `<`regex`>` part on the pattern.

This only returns capturing groups. So you will have to surround the regex in parentheses in the pattern if you want to read the entire captured pattern.

en:
The text input for a `<`regex`>` part on the pattern.

This only returns capturing groups. So you will have to surround the regex in parentheses in the pattern if you want to read the entire captured pattern.

syntax_2907.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2907.title: On Bukkit Event
syntax_2907.description:
You may listen to any Bukkit-based event (including events added by other plugins) by referencing the imported class

You may also listen to multiple events with the same handler. The events do not have to be related, but you should take appropriate precautions if you try to access methods that are available in one event but not in the other.

### Setting a Priority Level
The priority level of an event may be set to control when a particular event handler is run relative to other event handlers.
Any event priorities defined in [org.bukkit.event.EventPriority](https://hub.spigotmc.org/javadocs/bukkit/org/bukkit/event/EventPriority.html) may be used. Lower priority event handlers are run before higher priority event handlers.

* lowest
* low
* normal
* high
* highest
* monitor

❗ `highest` is the highest priority event handler you should use if you are modifying the contents of an event. If you only care about the final result of the event, use `monitor`.

### Handling Cancelled Events
By default, event handlers will not be called if an event is cancelled by a lower priority handler. This behavior can be changed by specifying that the handler should handle `all` events.

en:
You may listen to any Bukkit-based event (including events added by other plugins) by referencing the imported class

You may also listen to multiple events with the same handler. The events do not have to be related, but you should take appropriate precautions if you try to access methods that are available in one event but not in the other.

### Setting a Priority Level
The priority level of an event may be set to control when a particular event handler is run relative to other event handlers.
Any event priorities defined in [org.bukkit.event.EventPriority](https://hub.spigotmc.org/javadocs/bukkit/org/bukkit/event/EventPriority.html) may be used. Lower priority event handlers are run before higher priority event handlers.

* lowest
* low
* normal
* high
* highest
* monitor

❗ `highest` is the highest priority event handler you should use if you are modifying the contents of an event. If you only care about the final result of the event, use `monitor`.

### Handling Cancelled Events
By default, event handlers will not be called if an event is cancelled by a lower priority handler. This behavior can be changed by specifying that the handler should handle `all` events.

syntax_2906.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2906.title: Import
syntax_2906.description:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to import classes.

```skript
import:
    <fully qualified name> [as <alias>]
```

Once you import a class using this section, skript-mirror will create an expression allowing you to reference the Java class by its simple name or alias. Also these expressions are local to the script to avoid conflicts, so you must import java classes in each script that uses them.

##### Dealing with nested classes
Sometimes, a class may be nested inside another class. When referring to the fully qualified name of the class, the nested class is separated from the surrounding class using a `$` rather than a `.`

For example, `org.bukkit.entity.EnderDragon.Phase` would become `org.bukkit.entity.EnderDragon$Phase`.

Nested classes usually have more general names than their surrounding classes, so you should import these under an alias.

en:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to import classes.

```skript
import:
    <fully qualified name> [as <alias>]
```

Once you import a class using this section, skript-mirror will create an expression allowing you to reference the Java class by its simple name or alias. Also these expressions are local to the script to avoid conflicts, so you must import java classes in each script that uses them.

##### Dealing with nested classes
Sometimes, a class may be nested inside another class. When referring to the fully qualified name of the class, the nested class is separated from the surrounding class using a `$` rather than a `.`

For example, `org.bukkit.entity.EnderDragon.Phase` would become `org.bukkit.entity.EnderDragon$Phase`.

Nested classes usually have more general names than their surrounding classes, so you should import these under an alias.

syntax_2905.creator: Blueyescat
syntax_2905.title: Define Expression
syntax_2905.description:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to create custom expressions.

Use the first pattern if you want to create an expression with only one pattern, and use the second pattern for multiple patterns (then `patterns` section is used to enter the patterns.

Use the last pattern to create the following patterns easily.
```skript
[the] <pattern> of %skript types%
%skript types%'[s] <pattern>
```
---

The `local` part on the patterns makes the expression local to the current script, so it can't be used from any other script.

Specifying `plural` or `non-single` on the pattern indicates that the expression may return more than one value regardless of context.

Also **`$`** type prefix can be used to make the expression single or non-single depending on whether the input is single or non-single.

**For example:** `expression uppercase %$strings%:`

`uppercase "test"` would be single and `uppercase "hello" and "world"` would be non-single.


---
(Optional) a `parse` section can be used to run some code at the parse time of this expression. If this section is included, you must also [continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the expression was parsed successfully. Local variables created in this section are copied by-value to other sections.

`loop of` entry can be used to create a custom loop value for non-single expressions. For example `loop of: point` would be `loop-point` when looping the expression.

(Optional) a `return type` entry can be used to specify the return type of this expression.

A `get` section can be used to return something from this expression (using the [Return](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2914) effect).

---
`set`, `add`, `remove`, `remove all` , `delete` and `reset` sections can be used to add changers to this expression.
Required type of change values can be specified using `<changer> %skript type%[, <more>]` (see the examples)

Also check out the [Change Values](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2917) expression.

en:
Actually this is not an event, but a top-level section. Used to create custom expressions.

Use the first pattern if you want to create an expression with only one pattern, and use the second pattern for multiple patterns (then `patterns` section is used to enter the patterns.

Use the last pattern to create the following patterns easily.
```skript
[the] <pattern> of %skript types%
%skript types%'[s] <pattern>
```
---

The `local` part on the patterns makes the expression local to the current script, so it can't be used from any other script.

Specifying `plural` or `non-single` on the pattern indicates that the expression may return more than one value regardless of context.

Also **`$`** type prefix can be used to make the expression single or non-single depending on whether the input is single or non-single.

**For example:** `expression uppercase %$strings%:`

`uppercase "test"` would be single and `uppercase "hello" and "world"` would be non-single.


---
(Optional) a `parse` section can be used to run some code at the parse time of this expression. If this section is included, you must also [continue](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2910) **if** the expression was parsed successfully. Local variables created in this section are copied by-value to other sections.

`loop of` entry can be used to create a custom loop value for non-single expressions. For example `loop of: point` would be `loop-point` when looping the expression.

(Optional) a `return type` entry can be used to specify the return type of this expression.

A `get` section can be used to return something from this expression (using the [Return](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2914) effect).

---
`set`, `add`, `remove`, `remove all` , `delete` and `reset` sections can be used to add changers to this expression.
Required type of change values can be specified using `<changer> %skript type%[, <more>]` (see the examples)

Also check out the [Change Values](https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=2917) expression.
